exemplar.title: How does current affect force on an insulated copper wire in a magnetic field?

detailedExemplarData.title: How does current affect force on an insulated copper wire in a magnetic field?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Tables and graphs consistently carry units, uncertainties, and error bars; axes are labelled and a bestâfit equation is presented, making data communication both clear and precise.
Uncertainties from measuring devices are identified and propagated correctly for most variables; the gradient uncertainty is obtained by a sound maxâmin method.
Processed data tables clearly show means and propagated uncertainties, and the graph presentation is clean and professionally formatted.
weaknesses: Uncertainty in the gravitational constant g is omitted and should be justified or included.
Propagated uncertainty for the wire length L is missing from the processedâdata table, reducing consistency.
A unit conversion slip in the sample calculation (g to kg) and inconsistent rounding in the spreadsheet introduce minor inaccuracies.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two of the three strands achieve the top band (5â6) and the third is at the upper end of the 3â4 band.  Overall, data recording, processing, and uncertainty analysis are executed with clarity and precision, meriting a strong 5.  Minor omissions and calculation lapses prevent a full 6.

strengths: The research question is embedded within a clear theoretical background and specific experimental setup, providing a wellâdefined context for investigation.

The methodology descriptionâincluding apparatus list, circuit diagram, setâup sketch, and a numbered procedure with ranges, step sizes, trial counts, and calibration stepsâallows for reproducibility with minimal ambiguity.
Methodological considerations such as wire length, field strength, angle, air currents, and heating are each explained, demonstrating thoughtful control and justification of data sufficiency.
weaknesses: The specific field strength of the horseshoe magnet is not stated, reducing full reproducibility.
References to current ranges as âroughlyâ introduce ambiguity; exact step increments (e.g. 0.50 A) should be specified.
Equipment details such as powerâpack EMF and model numbers are omitted, leaving minor gaps in methodological clarity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments place this work solidly at the 5â6 level: the research question sits in a specific context, the methodology is largely reproducible, and methodological considerations are explained.  Small omissions in field strength and equipment details prevent a topâband 6, so a score of 5 accurately reflects the overall quality.

strengths: The conclusion restates the observed linear relationship and references gradient/intercept values, linking results back to the original hypothesis.
An anomaly at 1 A is identified and discussed with reference to the graph, showing engagement with unexpected data.
weaknesses: Comparison to the accepted scientific context is superficial; percentage deviation and detailed errorâsource discussion are not provided.
The anomaly discussion would be strengthened by quantitative residual analysis or percentâdifference calculations.
The conclusion is consistent with analysis but lacks deeper quantitative justification required for the top band.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The first strand (relevance and support) meets the 5â6 description but is slightly brief; the second strand (scientific context) is in the 3â4 band.  Taken together, the conclusion is coherent and supported but not fully justified against scientific context, warranting a score of 4.

strengths: Realistic and relevant improvements are described in detail (e.g. wind shielding, alignment with a digital gauge, insulated clips).
The evaluation proposes a meaningful extension (varying angle, using a gaussmeter), showing creative foresight beyond basic corrections.
weaknesses: Although specific methodological weaknesses are described, their relative impact on total uncertainty is not quantified or ranked.
The significance of each error source lacks comparative assessment, which would clarify which factors dominate overall uncertainty.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation identifies specific weaknesses (strand 3â4) and provides realistic, relevant improvements (strand 3â4).  While it does not explain the relative impact of each limitation, the level of detail in proposed improvements pushes performance to the top of the 3â4 band.  A score of 4 reflects this balance.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is clearly stated at the very start in interrogative form and is immediately framed by a concise theoretical background on magnetic forces on current-carrying conductors.  The subsequent paragraphs link the situation to Flemingâs rule, the right-hand rule and the specific horseshoe-magnet / balance setup that will be used, giving a concrete experimental context rather than a purely generic one.  Because the reader is told exactly what kind of wire, field and measurement technique are involved, the question sits in a âspecific and appropriateâ context.  Minor gaps (e.g. not naming field strength of chosen magnet) prevent full excellence, so a solid 5 rather than 6 is awarded.

Uncertainties of measuring devices are identified, mean-range/2 is used, error bars plotted, gradient uncertainty obtained by maxâmin method, and propagation to B is shown.  Omission of uncertainty in g and partial treatment of L leave small but noticeable gaps; therefore 5 marks.

Relevant methodological considerations are not merely listed but **explained**: wire length, field strength, angle, air currents and heating are each linked to their physical effect on force.  Controls (clips, clamps, permanent magnet) and justification of five-trial averaging show the student has thought about obtaining *sufficient* data.  The discussion is thorough but some controls (temperature of magnet, balance drift) are not addressed, so 5/6 is appropriate.

Four concrete weaknesses are identified and their qualitative effects outlined; significance is rated, but the explanation of *relative* impact (which sources dominate total uncertainty) is thin.  Hence the strand fits the âdescribes specific weaknessesâ descriptor, earning 3.

The apparatus list, circuit diagram, set-up sketch and numbered procedure allow a competent student to reproduce the investigation without further clarification.  Ranges, step size, number of trials (five) and calibration steps are all specified.  Only small ambiguities remain (exact power-pack EMF, model numbers), so the level corresponds to the top band; a score of 5 is given to reflect those minor omissions.

Averages, force conversion, linear fit and gradient extraction are correct in principle.  However a unit slip is evident in the sample calculation (1.8 g Ã 9.8 giving 0.0176 N) and the spreadsheet rounds some forces inconsistently.  Despite these inaccuracies, the trend analysis is still largely sound, warranting the 3-4 band; the quality is closer to the upper end, so 4 marks are awarded.

For each weakness a realistic and relevant improvement is provided (wind shielding, alignment markings, precision bending, insulated clips).  Although some suggestions remain qualitative, they clearly go beyond simple statements and describe how to implement changes, satisfying the 3-4 band; quality is nearer the top so 4 marks are assigned.

Raw tables carry units, uncertainties and trial structure; processed table clearly shows means and propagated uncertainties; graphs include labelled axes, error bars and a fit equation.  Presentation is clean but the wide table is awkward to read and some headings wrap poorly, hence 5 rather than 6.

The student compares the measured 0.031 T with a manufacturerâs 0.06 T value and notes it is roughly half, offering a qualitative interpretation of magnet weakness.  No deeper discussion of percentage difference or error sources is given, so this is a *relevant* but not *fully justified* comparison, meriting the 3-4 band at 4 marks.

The conclusion restates the linear relationship and cites both the gradient and the near-zero intercept as supporting evidence; the anomaly at 1 A is discussed in light of the graph.  The logic is consistent with the full data set and directly references analysis, justifying a 5.  Slight brevity in quantitative referencing prevents full 6.

annotations.comment: The research question is embedded within a clear theoretical background and specific experimental setup, providing a wellâdefined context for investigation.
The background description of permanent magnets omits the specific field strength of the horseshoe magnet used; include this value to strengthen reproducibility.
The circuit diagram (Figure 2.1) is clear and wellâlabelled, greatly aiding the reproducibility of the experimental setup.
Consider specifying the power-pack EMF and equipment model numbers in the procedure to remove remaining ambiguities and ensure full reproducibility.
The gravitational field strength g is given as 9.8 N kgâ»Â¹ without any uncertainty; include or justify the omission of Îg when propagating uncertainties.
Describing the current range as âroughlyâ reduces precision; specify exact step increments (e.g. 0.50 A) and record measured values directly for clarity.
The controlled-variables table headings wrap over multiple lines, which impedes readability; consider reformatting or splitting the table.
The identification of uncontrollable variables (wire resistance and air disturbances) shows thoughtful consideration of factors affecting data quality.
Step 3âs instruction to set the current âroughlyâ introduces ambiguity; instruct to adjust the resistor until the ammeter reads the exact desired value before recording.
The raw data table is comprehensive but spans too many columns; consider splitting or rotating the table to improve clarity of data recording.
The processed-data table omits the propagated uncertainty for the wire length L; include ÎL in the uncertainty column to be consistent.
The sample calculation uses  â1.8 (g) directly in F=mg without converting to kilograms; it should be F=â0.0018 kgÃ9.8 Nâkgâ»Â¹ = â0.0176 N.
The average-uncertainty calculation using range/2 is clearly demonstrated, providing a solid basis for further propagation.
The uncertainty calculation at zero current divides by zero, which is undefined; instead assign the instrumentâs resolution (Â±0.01) directly for ÎF at zero.
The plotted graph includes error bars, labeled axes, and a bestâfit line, presenting data processing that is both clear and precise.
Present the intercept in conventional scientific format (e.g. â3.64Ã10â»âµ) for clarity, rather than E notation with ambiguous spacing.
The uncertainty of the graphâs gradient is correctly determined by maxâmin/2, demonstrating appropriate consideration of uncertainties.
The conclusion effectively restates how the analysis supports the hypothesis, offering a justified and consistent summary of results.
The anomaly discussion is insightful, but consider quantifying the deviation using residuals or percent difference to strengthen the interpretation.
The significance column in the evaluation lacks a comparative assessment of which errors dominate total uncertainty; include relative rankings or quantitative estimates.
For precise 90Â° wire alignment, use a protractor or digital angle gauge rather than relying solely on pliers and an edge.
The extension proposes varying the angle and using a gaussmeter, offering realistic and relevant improvements to further explore the limitations.
Some references lack full DOI or consistent retrieval dates; ensure all citations follow a uniform format and include necessary identifiers.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â

similarExemplars.title: I will investigate the relationship between the enclosed current in a solenoid (27.1mA, 40.2mA, 53.7mA, 80.4mA, 106.7mA), and the magnetic field strength produced by that solenoid (mT). The solenoid will be consistent each trial, consisting of an insulated copper wire wrapped 60 times around an iron core that has a radius of 1cm and length of 15cm.

userSubmission.researchQuestion: I will investigate the relationship between the enclosed current in a solenoid (27.1mA, 40.2mA, 53.7mA, 80.4mA, 106.7mA), and the magnetic field strength produced by that solenoid (mT). The solenoid will be consistent each trial, consisting of an insulated copper wire wrapped 60 times around an iron core that has a radius of 1cm and length of 15cm.

similarExemplars.title: How does the amount of current running through a solenoid with an iron core affect the magnetic flux density through it?

userSubmission.researchQuestion: How does the amount of current running through a solenoid with an iron core affect the magnetic flux density through it?

similarExemplars.title: To what extent does the number of turns in a coil (50, 100, 150, 200) affect the strength of the magnetic field it produces, when varying voltages (1.5V, 3V, 4.5V, 6V, 9V, 12V) are applied across it?

userSubmission.researchQuestion: To what extent does the number of turns in a coil (50, 100, 150, 200) affect the strength of the magnetic field it produces, when varying voltages (1.5V, 3V, 4.5V, 6V, 9V, 12V) are applied across it?
